notes.note: "Your friend" is sarcasm for Antony, Cassius's bitter enemy; furor (frenzy) is a recurring charge in the Philippics.

notes.anchor: the statue he set up on the Rostra
notes.note: Antony erected a statue on the Rostra (the speaker's platform in the Forum) honoring the murdered Caesar, a public provocation to the assassins.
The inscription (Latin PARENTI OPTIME MERITO) styles Caesar the "parent" of his country; it implies that those who killed him are guilty of parricide, the murder of a father.

notes.anchor: not merely assassins but actually parricides
notes.note: A pointed escalation: sicarii (cutthroats) was bad enough, but the statue's inscription brands the Liberators parricidae, killers of the father of the fatherland, the gravest crime in Roman law and feeling.

notes.anchor: brought before a public meeting by Cannutius
notes.note: A contio was an informal public assembly summoned by a magistrate; the tribune Ti. Cannutius, an enemy of Antony, hauled him before one where Antony came off badly.
Conservatores patriae, "preservers of the fatherland," was the Liberators' own claim for assassinating Caesar; Antony inverted it, calling them traitors instead.

notes.anchor: they have stripped your envoy of his travel-money
notes.note: The viaticum was the official allowance for an envoy's journey; confiscating it treated Cassius's representative, and Cassius himself, as agents of a public enemy (hostis).

notes.anchor: We could not bear a master; we are slaves to a fellow-slave
notes.note: A bitter epigram: having killed the dominus (Caesar the dictator), Rome now grovels before Antony, a mere conservus (fellow-slave) who was no better than they.
